title: Formal vs informal email language
description: Understand and practise the difference between formal and informal register in written English.

content.title: Register â€” formal vs informal
content.body:
REGISTER means the level of formality in your language. You choose based on WHO you are writing to.

FORMAL (to a boss, client, professor, stranger, company):
â€¢ Full sentences, full forms
â€¢ No slang, no emoji
â€¢ Polite and distant
â€¢ 'I am writing to request...'

INFORMAL (to a friend, close colleague, family):
â€¢ Contractions, casual phrases
â€¢ Can use emoji, exclamation marks
â€¢ Friendly and warm
â€¢ 'Hey! Just wanted to check...'

SEMI-FORMAL (to a known colleague you're friendly with):
â€¢ Mix of both â€” friendly but professional
content.grammar_note:
Register mistakes = mismatches
Writing to your boss like a friend = too casual
Writing to your friend like a formal letter = awkward

content.title: Formal vs informal phrase comparison
content.body:
INFORMAL â†’ FORMAL

â€¢ Hey! â†’ Dear [Name],
â€¢ Just wanted to say â†’ I am writing to inform you
â€¢ Can you...? â†’ Could you please...? / I would be grateful if you could...
â€¢ Sorry for the late reply â†’ I apologise for the delay in responding
â€¢ Thanks! â†’ Thank you for your time
â€¢ No problem â†’ It is not an inconvenience
â€¢ I'll do it â†’ I will ensure it is completed
â€¢ ASAP â†’ at your earliest convenience
â€¢ FYI â†’ For your information / Please note that
â€¢ Let me know â†’ Please do not hesitate to contact me
â€¢ Bye! / Cheers â†’ Yours sincerely / Kind regards
content.grammar_note:
Abbreviations like FYI, ASAP, BTW are informal only
Never use them in formal emails

vocabulary.example: All correspondence should be kept on file.
Please acknowledge receipt of this email.

vocabulary.meaning: to send a received email to someone else
vocabulary.example: I will forward the email to the relevant team.

vocabulary.meaning: carbon copy â€” to send a copy to another person
vocabulary.example: Please cc the manager on all client emails.
This is urgent â€” please reply today.

sentences: I apologise for the delay in responding to your email.
Could you please confirm your availability for a meeting?
Please note that the deadline has been extended to Friday.
I will ensure the report is completed by end of day.
Thank you for bringing this matter to my attention.

content.question: Priya is writing to a potential employer. Which greeting is correct?
content.explanation: A potential employer = formal. 'Dear Mr [Name]' is the correct formal greeting.

content.question: Which sentence is appropriate in a formal email?
options: Sorry for the late reply â€” things got crazy!
I apologise for the delay in my response.
Oops, forgot to reply â€” my bad!
content.correct: I apologise for the delay in my response.
content.explanation: Formal English: full sentences, full words, polite phrasing.

content.question: Kavya is messaging her close friend about weekend plans. Which is appropriate?
options: Dear Meera, I am writing to enquire about your availability this weekend.
Hey Meera! Are you free Saturday? ðŸ˜Š
To Whom It May Concern, I wish to discuss weekend plans.
I would be grateful if you could confirm your weekend availability.
content.correct: Hey Meera! Are you free Saturday? ðŸ˜Š
content.explanation: Writing to a close friend = informal. Casual greetings, contractions, and emoji are all fine here.

content.question: Which sign-off is appropriate for a formal email to a client?
content.explanation: 'Best regards' + full name = professional and appropriate for a client email.

content.instruction: Rewrite the informal phrase as a formal one.

sentences.text: Can you send it ASAP? â†’ Could you please send it ___ ___?
Sorry, forgot to reply â†’ I ___ for the delay in responding.
FYI the meeting is moved. â†’ ___ ___ ___ the meeting has been rescheduled.
Let me know if you need anything. â†’ Please do not ___ to contact me.

content.instruction: Is each email sentence FORMAL (F) or INFORMAL (I)? Write F or I.

sentences.text: I am writing to inform you of a change in schedule.
Hey! Just checking â€” still on for tomorrow?
I would be grateful if you could review the attached document.
Can't make it today â€” something came up ðŸ˜…
I look forward to hearing from you at your earliest convenience.

content.instruction: Arrange into a formal apology sentence.
content.answer: I apologise for the delay in responding.
I would be grateful if you could confirm your attendance.

content.instruction: Match the informal phrase to its formal equivalent.

pairs.right: I apologise for the delay in responding
I look forward to hearing from you.

content.title: Two emails â€” same message, different registers
content.setting: Ravi needs to tell his team about a meeting change. He writes two versions â€” one to his best friend on the team, and one to his director.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi (to friend Ankit â€” informal)
dialogue.text: Hey Ankit! Quick heads up â€” the team meeting tomorrow has moved to 3 PM instead of 11. Something came up on the director's side. Let me know if that works for you. See you then! ðŸ˜Š

dialogue.speaker: Ankit
dialogue.text: No worries! 3 PM works fine. Thanks for letting me know ðŸ‘

dialogue.speaker: Ravi (to Director Sharma â€” formal)
dialogue.text: Dear Mr Sharma, I am writing to inform you that the team meeting scheduled for tomorrow at 11 AM has been rescheduled to 3 PM. I apologise for any inconvenience this may cause. Please let me know if this time is convenient for you. I look forward to your confirmation. Kind regards, Ravi Krishnan

dialogue.speaker: Director Sharma
dialogue.text: Dear Ravi, thank you for the update. 3 PM works for me. Please send a revised calendar invite.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Dear Mr Sharma, thank you for confirming. I will send the updated calendar invite shortly. Kind regards, Ravi

dialogue.speaker: Ravi (to Ankit â€” informal again)
dialogue.text: Director confirmed! 3 PM it is. I'll update the calendar. This meeting better be short though ðŸ˜‚

dialogue.speaker: Ankit
dialogue.text: Haha agreed! See you tomorrow ðŸ™Œ

comprehension_questions.question: What changed about the meeting?
options: It moved from 11 AM to 3 PM.
It moved to a different day.
comprehension_questions.correct: It moved from 11 AM to 3 PM.

comprehension_questions.question: Which phrase does Ravi use in the formal email to apologise?
options: I apologise for any inconvenience this may cause.
comprehension_questions.correct: I apologise for any inconvenience this may cause.

comprehension_questions.question: What does the Director ask Ravi to do?

content.instruction: These formal email extracts have register mistakes. Correct them.

sentences.wrong: Hey! I'm writing about the invoice I sent last week.
sentences.correct: Dear [Name], I am writing regarding the invoice I submitted last week.
sentences.explanation: 'Hey' is informal. Use 'Dear'. Also use full form 'I am' and formal verb 'submitted'.

sentences.wrong: Can you get back to me ASAP?
sentences.correct: I would appreciate a response at your earliest convenience.
sentences.explanation: 'ASAP' is informal/abbreviation. 'Can you' is too casual for formal emails.
'Cheers' and emoji are informal. Use 'Kind regards' or 'Yours sincerely' + full name.

content.scenario: You need to email your university's admissions office to ask about scholarship options. Which opening is most appropriate?
options: Hey! I wanted to ask about scholarships.
Dear Admissions Office, I am writing to enquire about scholarship opportunities available for the 2025-26 academic year.
To whoever's reading this â€” I need scholarship info.
Hi, can you tell me about scholarships?
content.correct: Dear Admissions Office, I am writing to enquire about scholarship opportunities available for the 2025-26 academic year.
content.explanation: An official university department = formal register. Clear, specific, and polite.

content.instruction: Compare these two situations and explain what register you would use and why. Then give 3 example phrases for each.
content.prompt: Situation A: Emailing a new client about a project proposal. Situation B: Messaging your close colleague to say you'll be 10 minutes late. What register? What phrases?
content.example_response:
For Situation A â€” emailing a new client â€” I would use formal register because I don't know them personally and this is a professional business context. I would use phrases like: 'Dear Mr/Ms [Name],' to open, 'I am writing to present our proposal for the upcoming project' for the opening line, and 'I look forward to your feedback at your earliest convenience' to close. I would not use contractions or abbreviations.

For Situation B â€” messaging my close colleague â€” I would use informal register. We know each other well, this is a quick update, and it's through messaging, not email. I'd write something like: 'Hey! Running 10 mins late â€” sorry!' or 'On my way â€” traffic is bad, be there by 9:15' or just 'Late by 10 â€” sorry!' with maybe a quick emoji. The context matters a lot â€” the same information is communicated very differently depending on who you're talking to.

content.instruction: Write and say aloud TWO short emails: one formal, one informal â€” both saying the same thing. The message: you need to cancel a plan for this Friday.
content.prompt: Say aloud: (1) A formal email to a business contact cancelling a Friday meeting. (2) An informal message to a friend cancelling Friday plans.
content.example_response:
Here are both versions:

Formal email: 'Subject: Cancellation of Friday Meeting â€” 18th October. Dear Ms Kapoor, I am writing to inform you that I am unfortunately unable to attend our meeting scheduled for Friday, 18th October, due to an urgent personal commitment. I sincerely apologise for any inconvenience this may cause. I would be grateful if we could reschedule at a mutually convenient time. Please let me know your availability for next week. Kind regards, Arjun Mehta.'

Informal message to a friend: 'Hey Meera! Really sorry but I have to cancel our Friday plans â€” something urgent came up with my family. Can we reschedule to next weekend? I'll make it up to you, promise! ðŸ™ Let me know what works for you.'
key_phrases: I am writing to inform you

content.instruction: Read each context and choose FORMAL or INFORMAL phrasing for each gap.
content.text:
Context: Ravi is writing to his college professor (FORMAL).

Dear ___ (1) Patel,

I ___ (2) writing to request your guidance on the final year project topic. I have been ___ (3) two possible areas â€” Machine Learning and Data Visualisation â€” and I would value your expert opinion.

___ (4) you please advise me on which area would be more suitable given the department's current research focus?

I ___ (5) forward to your response. Please ___ (6) hesitate to let me know if you would like me to prepare any additional material beforehand.

___ (7) sincerely,
Ravi Sharma

content.title: Lesson 8 complete â€” Formal vs informal email
key_points: Register = the level of formality â€” choose based on your relationship with the reader
Formal: full forms, no slang, polite phrases, professional sign-offs
Informal: contractions, casual phrases, emoji allowed
Common switches: ASAP â†’ 'at your earliest convenience', FYI â†’ 'Please note that', Can you â†’ 'Could you please'
Mixing registers sounds unnatural â€” be consistent throughout the email
content.next_lesson: Making appointments â€” suggesting, accepting, and confirming times
